examples.title: Algorithm: Merge Sort
examples.code:
MERGE_SORT(list)
 IF length(list) <= 1 THEN
 RETURN list
 END IF
 mid = length(list) / 2
 left = MERGE_SORT(list[0..mid-1])
 right = MERGE_SORT(list[mid..length(list)-1])
 RETURN MERGE(left, right)
END

MERGE(left, right)
 result = []
 WHILE length(left) > 0 AND length(right) > 0
 IF left[0] <= right[0] THEN
 APPEND result, left[0]
 REMOVE left[0]
 ELSE
 APPEND result, right[0]
 REMOVE right[0]
 END IF
 END WHILE
 APPEND result, left
 APPEND result, right
 RETURN result
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the merge sort algorithm used by Collections.sort(). It recursively divides the list into two halves until each sublist contains only one element, then merges the sublists in sorted order.

examples.title: Java: Sorting with Collections.sort()
examples.description: Sorting a list of integers using Collections.sort()
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates how to sort a list of integers using Collections.sort(). The sorted list is then printed to the console.

examples.title: Java: Binary Search with Collections.binarySearch()
examples.description: Performing a binary search on a sorted list using Collections.binarySearch()
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates how to perform a binary search on a sorted list using Collections.binarySearch(). The index of the target element is printed to the console.

examples.title: C: Merge Sort Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of the merge sort algorithm in C
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates the implementation of the merge sort algorithm. The mergeSort function recursively divides the array into two halves until each subarray contains only one element, then merges the subarrays in sorted order using the merge function.
